Chemical structures of BRD4-BD2 selective inhibitors 39–58. (XY153) and compound 54, both of which have demonstrated promising antitumor effectiveness and potential for treating AML. Compound 54 has demonstrated good cardiovascular safety in both cell-based and animal models [94,95]. Inspired by penipanoid C, Chen et al. synthe- sized compound 55, a selective BRD4-BD2 inhibitor exhibiting anti- inflammatory activity [96]. Multiple institutions, including Nuvation Bio Inc., Centre Hospitalier Universitaire, and Tay Therapeutics Ltd., have disclosed various BD2- selective inhibitors in their patents. These compounds predominantly feature chemical scaffolds such as dihydropyrrolopyridinones, pyrrolo- pyridinones, and tetrahydroisoquinolinone scaffolds. For example, pat- ent WO 2022248475 describes tetrahydroquinoline (THQ)-based BRD4 inhibitors (56) [97], while WO 2024018423 presents BRD4 inhibitors incorporating pyrrole or imidazole as core structures (57) [98]. US11192900 reports pyrrolone derivatives (58) that exhibit enhanced kinetic stability and target selectivity [99]. 8

L. Deng et al. Results in Chemistry 18 (2025) 102670 ## 4.5. Dual/multi-target inhibitors In recent years, multi-target strategies have garnered significant attention in the development of anticancer therapeutics, particularly those that adopt polypharmacological approaches by targeting different proteins and inhibiting kinases [100]. It has been revealed in previous research that the CDK inhibitor Dinaciclib can bind to the KAc-binding pocket of BRDT, offering initial evidence for the potential repurposing of kinase inhibitors in the development of next-generation BRD4 inhibitors [101]. This discovery has inspired the development of a series of dual BET/kinase inhibitors [102,103]. Histone deacetylases (HDACs) reduce lysine acetylation, modulating BET protein binding to gene loci and influencing transcription [104,105]. Therefore, they are considered a rational strategy to suppress oncogene transcription and inhibit tumor cell proliferation more effectively [106,107]. A number of dual BET/HDAC inhibitors (59-64, Fig. 6) have demonstrated promising efficacy in the treatment of colorectal cancer (CRC) and AML, effectively suppressing the growth of BET inhibitor-resistant cells. These findings highlight the rising importance of dual-target drug development focusing on both BET proteins and HDACs [107-111]. For instance, compound 59 was designed and synthesized by combining (+)-JQ1 with HDAC inhibitors SAHA and CI-944 [112]. Structural modifications of already developed dual inhibitors can yield new derivatives with distinct biological activities. For example, com- pounds 66, 67, and 68 were optimized from the parent compound 65 [111,113] Other recently developed dual BET/HDAC inhibitors (69–73, Fig. 6) have followed similar design strategies, providing a broad reference framework for the study and development of this class of in- hibitors [114–116]. The inhibition of BRD4, in conjunction with poly(ADP-ribose) po-lymerase 1 (PARP1) inhibitors, has been shown to induce homologous recombination (HR) deficiency, offering a promising new approach to cancer treatment [117,118]. Leveraging synthetic lethality, compound 74 (Fig. 6) represents the first dual BRD4/PARP1 inhibitor reported to induce apoptosis and G1-phase arrest in breast cancer cells effectively [119]. Huang et al. developed a dual-target PARP1/BRD4 inhibitor, III-7 (75), by integrating the PARP1 inhibitor Veliparib with a BRD4 inhibi-tor, showing efficacy in pancreatic cancer treatment [120]. Zhang et al. reported the identification of BP44 (76), a 4-hydroxyquinazoline de-rivative, as a dual BET/PARP1 inhibitor demonstrating strong anti-proliferative activity in TNBC models [121]. It was revealed in another study that Polo-like kinase 1 (PLK1) forms a feed-forward loop with MYC through the upregulation of BRD4-controlled MYC transcription and protein stability [122]. Through chemical modification of the known BRD4/PLK1 dual inhibitor BI-2536 (65), WNY0824 (66), a new dual BET/PLK1 inhibitor created by modifying BI-2536 (65), effectively blocks BRD4-dependent androgen receptor signaling and MYC expres-sion. Compounds 67 and 68 were also identified using this structure-guided approach [113,123]. However, a critical design challenge re-mains in achieving balanced selectivity between BET proteins and spe-cific kinases; for example, some studies suggest that PLK1 inhibition, rather than BRD4, may be the primary driver of BI-2536's anticancer effects in MV4-11 cells [124]. Dual-target inhibitors with BET inhibitory activity have been serendipitously discovered. TG101209 and BI-2536, initially characterized as Polo-like kinase 1 (PLK1) and Janus kinase 2 (JAK2) inhibitors, respectively, were subsequently found to be strong BRD4 inhibitors [125]. Other kinase targets to consider include PI3K-AKT-mTOR pathway inhibitors, which control MYC expression, and CDK9 inhibitors, which enhance transcriptional elongation by phosphorylating Ser2 on RNA polymerase II's CTD [7,32,126]. Additionally, Casein Kinase 2 (CK2) aids in chromatin targeting and cofactor recruitment by phosphorylating BRD4. Gene regulation is collaboratively controlled by both Enhancer of Zeste Homolog 2 (EZH2) and BRD4, making them promising targets for developing dual-inhibitors in cancer therapy treatment [33,127]. Results in Chemistry 18 (2025) 102670 based on EPZ6438 (EZH2 inhibitor) and JQ1 (BRD4 inhibitor) led to the design of YM458 (77), which significantly inhibited the proliferation of various solid tumor cell lines [127]. Likewise, compound ZLD-2 (78), derived from the fusion of GSK126 (EZH2 inhibitor) and JQ1, demon- strated dual inhibitory activity against EZH2 and BRD4 [128]. A range of dual and multi-target inhibitors — including BRD4/CK2 dual inhibitor 44e (79), BRD4/NAMPT (Nicotinamide Phosphoribosyl- transferase) dual inhibitor A2 (80), BRD4/Src dual inhibitor HL403 (81), BRD4/CDK6 (Cyclin-Dependent Kinase 6) dual inhibitor BC13 (82), BRD4/CDK4 (Cyclin-Dependent Kinase 4)/6 dual inhibitor B15 (83), and the Fedratinib-based HDAC/JAK (Janus Kinase)/BRD4 triple inhibitor 25ap (84) — have shown promising therapeutic efficacy across multiple cancer models [129-134]. 4.6. PROTACS PROTACs (Proteolysis Targeting Chimeras) are an emerging strategy for functional protein suppression that operates through compound-mediated proteolysis. This approach exhibits unique pharmacological characteristics, including potent antiproliferative effects and reduced toxicity [1,19,22,135]. Most of the BET degraders developed to date function by harnessing ligands that engage the E3 ubiquitin ligase components Cereblon (CRBN) or Von Hippel–Lindau (VHL). ARV-771 (85, Fig. 7) is a pan-BET degrader that recruits CRBN and has been shown to downregulate androgen receptor (AR) signaling proteins in a CRPC xenograft model. It represents the first BET degrader reported for solid tumors [136]. MZ1 (86), a JQ1-based BRD4 degrader, was further optimized into the macrocyclic PROTAC compound macroPROTAC-1 (87) by incorporating a cyclized linker into the ARV-771 scaffold, resulting in comparable cellular potency [137,138]. dBET1 (88), another selective BET degrader, exhibits stronger inhibition than JQ1 in various cell lines, including MV4-11 [139]. Several additional JQ1-based PROTACs, such as AGB1 (89), MZP-54 (90), MZP-55 (91), and MZP-61 (92), have demonstrated high efficacy in inducing BET protein degradation [140,141]. Non-selective BET inhibitors combined with CRBN ligands have also been used to develop BRD4-selective degraders such as BD-7148 (93) and BD-9136 (94), which achieve high specificity for BRD4 [142]. Other BET degraders, including ARV-825 (95), BETd-246 (96), BETD-260 (97), DP1 (98), QCA570 (99), and L134 (100), have shown potent efficacy in models of lymphoblasts, xenografted lymphomas, and breast cancer [19,79,143-145]. Notably, ZXH-3-26 (101) and AT1 (102) are characterized by their negligible activity against BRD2 and BRD3 at optimal concentrations, enhancing their BRD4 selectivity [146,147]. Dual-target BRD4 PROTACs offer promising new directions in drug discovery. For instance, BRD4-PROTAC WWL0245 (103), developed from the dual BET/PLK1 inhibitor WNY0824, displays strong anticancer activity in AR-positive prostate cancer cells. DP-15 (104), targeting both BRD4 and GSPT1, is effective against AML and non-Hodgkin lymphoma models [148,149]. While demonstrating significant promise through low-dose, durable target inhibition, the clinical translation of PROTACS faces challenges. These include reliance on the ubiquitin-proteasome system (UPS), po- tential for efflux by transporters like ABCB1, adaptive resistance mechanisms such as WNT-MYC pathway bypass, and the hook effect limiting the therapeutic window. To overcome these hurdles, active research is exploring innovative strategies, including the development of molecular glues, nano-responsive delivery systems, and optimized cat- alytic warheads/ligands [150-152]. Antibody-PROTAC conjugates (Ab- PROTACS) offer a promising strategy for tissue-specific BRD4 degrada- tion. For example, a trastuzumab-PROTAC conjugate can release the active PROTAC selectively upon linker cleavage, providing proof-of- concept for targeted degradation [153]. Selective degradation of BET proteins involves multiple pathways, including the ubiq- uitin-proteasome system as well as lysosomal and autophagy mecha- nisms [154]. <::Chemical structure of 85(ARV-771), a PROTAC degrader, showing a thienotriazolopyrimidine BRD4-binding moiety linked via an amide and ether chain to a phthalimide-based E3 ligase recruiting moiety. Protein-protein interaction networks Protein-protein interactions (PPIs) serve as crucial signaling junctions within molecular networks of normal and cancer cells, mediating the flow of pathophysiological information. Targeting these interactions to disrupt PPIs has thus emerged as a promising strategy to impede network signaling [155]. From a BRD38500-derived peptide combinatorial library, two peptide-like ligands—DC-1 (105) and DC-2 (106) 10

L. Deng et al. *Results in Chemistry 18 (2025) 102670* (Fig. 7)—were identified that bind selectively to a phosphorylated \u03b2 motif within human BRD4 [156]. These discoveries offer novel tem- plates for PPI-targeted BRD4 inhibitor development [35,157]. Addi- tionally, the irreversible PPI inhibitor 48 (107) has been shown to block the interaction between BRD4-BD1 and tetra-acetylated histone H4 peptides, thereby suppressing BRD4-driven transcription of MYC and BCL-2 genes [158]. Despite these promising leads, PPI-directed targeting of BRD4 remains underexplored. The main reasons include difficulties in confirming in vivo targets and action mechanisms, as well as obstacles in setting up effective high-throughput screening systems for PPI inhibitors [159]. ## 5. Conclusion and future perspectives To conclude, the development of BRD4 inhibitors has progressed significantly, with many institutions filing patents that reflect strong engagement in this field. Selective inhibitors targeting BRD4-BD1/BD2 and dual-target BRD4 inhibitors have gained prominence over pan-BET inhibitors as key research priorities. Both clinical and in vitro data demonstrate that selective inhibitors disrupt only disease-induced aberrant transcription with minimal impact on basal gene expression, thereby improving the therapeutic index and offering a more tolerable strategy for long-term treatment of non-oncologic conditions such as cardiovascular and metabolic diseases [160]. The development of BRD4-selective inhibitors, however, remains in its infancy [161,162]. The design of more selective inhibitors is becoming increasingly feasible due to enhanced understanding of the structural biology of BET proteins. Advances in protein structure prediction technologies now allow the simulation and refinement of protein conformations to better approximate native structures. These tools can be used to explore whether and how small molecules interact with BRD4 domains, providing new strategies for the rational design of selective inhibitors [163,164]. PROTAC and PPI technologies offer promising alternatives for targeting BRD4. PROTACs enable potent, sustained degradation at low doses, although their clinical translation presents significant challenges. Realizing the full potential of these innovative approaches requires overcoming these hurdles through continued collaborative efforts. # CRediT authorship contribution statement **Linlin Deng**: Writing review & editing, Writing original draft, Methodology. **Erkang Tian**: Supervision, Formal analysis, Conceptual- ization. **Ting Ma**: Formal analysis, Data curation. **Shaojie Liang**: Vali- dation, Investigation, Conceptualization. **Sijia Hu**: Methodology. **Linwei Li**: Writing - original draft, Methodology. **Juan Li**: Writing review & editing, Supervision.
